# Optimizing a Flat AI-Token Budget Across Unequal Users

**Final report — token-optim project.** All numbers below are generated by the
committed code (`RESULTS.md` via `python benchmark.py`, sweep tables via
`python alternatives.py`); 30 seeds per cell, paired comparisons, 95%
t-confidence intervals, 167 automated tests.

## 1. The problem

An organization buys a flat budget of `B` tokens per period. Usage is
heavy-tailed — with the calibrated default (lognormal σ=1.6), the top 20% of
users want ~78% of the tokens. The goal: heavy, experienced users get what
they need from the slack light users predictably leave, without raising `B`,
without heavy users robbing each other, and without locking out a light user
who ramps up.

Industry practice (LiteLLM, Kong AI gateway, Azure API Management) is static
per-user quotas — the thing this project set out to beat.

## 2. What we built and what the data did to it

The project went through one full falsification cycle — the final design is
not the one we started with, and that is the strongest evidence it's right.

**v1 (DAFB)** forecast each user's demand (EWMA + deviation headroom +
TCP-style saturation probing to uncensor capped users) and pre-assigned
quotas by entitlement floors plus utilization-weighted water-filling. It
crushed deployed practice (heavy-user unmet demand −44 to −54 pp vs equal
split, −28 to −35 pp vs static tiers, all significant) **but lost to its own
ablation** — plain water-filling with the same liquid buffer — by 7.7–9.7 pp
everywhere. Diagnosis: every token moved from the demand-revealing buffer
into an error-prone forecast is allocated strictly worse. The buffer, not
the forecasting, is the load-bearing mechanism.

**v2 (DAFB-v2, the final algorithm)** inverts the architecture:

1. **Floors** — each user is guaranteed `min(EWMA(usage), 0.5·B/n)`: small,
   demand-capped, no headroom. They exist for predictability and
   no-starvation, not to serve peak demand.
2. **Pool** — everything else (≥ 50% of `B`) stays liquid.
3. **Weighted draws** — each period the pool is distributed against *realized*
   excess demand by weighted water-filling, where weights are earned from
   demonstrated utilization (`0.25 + 0.75·EWMA(used/granted)`). Experienced
   heavy users win contention; hoarders fill slower; burning to fake
   experience is bounded (see §4).

Properties verified by tests: exact budget conservation, floor guarantees,
no-starvation, zero-demand slack recycling, symmetric treatment of equal
heavy users.

## 3. Headline results

Heavy-user unmet demand (stable scenario, mean ± 95% CI):

| equal split | static tiers | usage-proportional | max-min water-fill | **DAFB-v2** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 71.0 ± 1.6 % | 52.5 ± 3.1 % | 17.7 ± 0.9 % | 9.7 ± 0.8 % | **6.0 ± 0.9 %** |

v2 also posts the lowest waste of any algorithm (2.2–2.5% of `B` vs 9–12% for
max-min, ~60% for equal split), the highest utilization (86–92%), and
tied-best Jain fairness (99.3–99.9%). Under an FCFS draw race it wins **all
four scenarios** including structural crunch. Results are stable across tail
heaviness σ ∈ [1.2, 2.0].

**Where v2 is not the answer (honest boundary):**

- **Tight draw caps.** If pool draws are limited to ~1× equal share per
  period, v2 collapses (+30–36 pp vs max-min) — heavy users can't pull enough
  through the pipe. With rate-limited overage, deploy forecast-based
  water-filling quotas instead. *The right architecture is conditional on
  your serving layer.*
- **Structural crunch.** When org-wide demand ≈ 1.8×B frictionless, max-min
  pre-assignment beats v2 by ~6 pp. No allocator fixes an under-provisioned
  budget; that is a purchasing decision.

## 4. Incentives: gaming is profitless vandalism

A strategic mid-tier user who inflates claims and burns every granted token
(up to 4× equal share/period) **gains nothing**: their served true demand
changes by ≈0 pp, often negative (burning displaces their own real work).
Under slack-recycling allocation they were already nearly fully served. The
real damage is collateral: up to +5.5 pp unmet demand for honest heavy users
and ~7% of `B` destroyed per period.

**The cap dial** (draw cap = m × equal share, dafb_v2):

| cap | honest heavy-unmet (stable) | burner collateral | comment |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1x | 59.6% | +0.00 pp | fully immune, cripplingly slow |
| 2x | 43.7% | +0.00 pp | immune, still slow |
| **4x** | **27.1%** | **+0.19 pp** | the knee — near-immunity, usable |
| 8x | 14.8% | +1.40 pp | fast, small leak |
| uncapped | 6.0% | +3.22 pp | fastest, vandalizable |

Recommendation: **uncapped draws + spend monitoring for high-trust orgs**
(burning is visible in any usage dashboard and benefits nobody, so social/
administrative enforcement is cheap); **4× cap as the default for low-trust
or compliance-bound orgs** (collateral < 0.6 pp in every scenario tested).

## 5. Alternative mechanisms evaluated

1. **Team-pooling hierarchy (simulated).** Splitting one 50-user pool into
   five 10-user pools with B/5 each costs **+21 to +24 pp** heavy-user unmet
   demand in every scenario (significant). Heavy-tailed slack only recycles
   at scale: a small pool is often all-heavy or all-light at once. **Keep one
   org-wide pool; make teams accounting labels, not allocation boundaries.**
2. **Internal surplus auctions (second-price).** Pros: true price discovery
   under contention; theoretically efficient. Cons: bidding overhead per
   period, requires internal currency users value (else bids are cheap talk —
   the same problem as self-reported demand), and §4 shows there is little
   strategic pressure for prices to relieve. v2's utilization-weighted draws
   approximate the auction's outcome with zero bidding overhead: weights are
   bids paid in demonstrated past work. Verdict: not worth the machinery at
   intra-org scale; revisit only for cross-budget (inter-BU) trading.
3. **Peer-to-peer token lending.** Recent theory ("Credit Fairness: Online
   Fairness in Shared Resource Pools", arXiv 2601.17944) formalizes
   lend-early-recoup-later. v2's pool already *is* implicit lending — light
   users' slack flows to heavy users now; the earned-weight mechanism is the
   repayment ledger (consistent utilizers gain priority). An explicit ledger
   adds bilateral debt tracking and default risk at period boundaries for
   marginal gain. Verdict: adopt only if users demand legible reciprocity;
   back-of-envelope: with top-20% concentration at 78%, explicit bilateral
   loans would churn ~30% of B/period through the ledger — significant
   bookkeeping for an outcome the pool already delivers.
4. **Model-tier arbitrage (back-of-envelope — the biggest lever found).**
   Allocation redistributes a fixed B; routing changes B's effective size.
   With light-task tokens routed to a tier ~5× cheaper (typical flagship vs
   mid-tier pricing gap), shifting 50% of light users' volume frees
   `0.5 × (1−0.78) × 0.8 ≈ 9%` of B; shifting 50% of all volume that does not
   genuinely need a frontier model frees ~40%. Even the conservative case exceeds every allocation
   gain measured between v2 and max-min. **Do this first; it is orthogonal to
   and compounds with v2.**
5. **Priority credits.** Spend accumulated credits for guaranteed throughput
   at peak (latency/QoS, not volume). Orthogonal to budget allocation — it
   prices *when*, not *how much*. Cleanly layerable on v2 by letting credits
   temporarily boost draw weight. Untested here; flagged as future work.

## 6. Deployment guidance

1. Buy enough budget that sustained demand < B (no allocator fixes crunch).
2. Route light tasks to cheaper model tiers first (~9–40% effective budget).
3. Run **one org-wide DAFB-v2 pool**: half-share demand-capped floors,
   liquid pool, utilization-weighted draws.
4. Set the draw cap by trust level: uncapped + monitoring, or 4× default.
5. If your gateway cannot do real-time pool draws, fall back to max-min
   water-filling quotas on EWMA forecasts — still −61 pp vs equal split.
6. Re-run the benchmark against your own usage telemetry: every result here
   is reproducible from seeded synthetic traces (`python benchmark.py`,
   `python alternatives.py`), and the simulator's σ is a parameter.

## 7. Limitations

- All workloads are synthetic; tail calibration is order-of-magnitude
  (public per-user enterprise token distributions don't exist). σ was swept,
  not measured.
- Within-period dynamics are simplified to one draw round per period.
- Single-resource model: tokens only, no model-tier or latency coupling.
- Strategic analysis covers one gamer archetype at three greed levels;
  coalition gaming and adaptive adversaries are untested.
